title: WSi weak link element with a non-sinusoidal current-phase relation
authors: Sarah Garcia Jones, Trevyn F. Q. Larson, Sai Pavan Chitta, Heli Vora, Varun Verma, Sae Woo Nam, José Aumentado, Jens Koch, Raymond W. Simmonds, András Gyenis
abstract: Nonlinearity is an essential ingredient for encoding quantum states with non-uniform energy spacing, implementing coherent quantum gates, reading out qubits, amplifying, and mixing electromagnetic signals. In this work, we demonstrate the nonlinear behavior of a constriction fabricated from an amorphous, high-kinetic inductance material, tungsten silicide, embedded in a three-dimensional RF-SQUID. We find that the results are consistent with the weak link behaving as a Josephson junction with a sawtooth-like current-phase relation or a quantum phase slip element. Finally, we measure relaxation times of the metastable, persistent-current states trapped in the local minima of the potential.
summary: This paper investigates the nonlinear properties of a tungsten silicide nanowire used as a weak link in a superconducting RF-SQUID. It demonstrates that the device follows a non-sinusoidal current-phase relation and shows that metastable flux states can be extremely long-lived. These findings suggest WSi is a promising material for developing new types of nonlinear superconducting quantum elements.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the nonlinear behavior and current-phase relation (CPR) of a tungsten silicide (WSi) nanowire weak link to determine if it is better described by a non-sinusoidal Josephson junction model or a quantum phase slip (QPS) model.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that the WSi weak link exhibits a non-sinusoidal, sawtooth-like CPR and found that both the skewed Josephson junction and QPS models can describe low-energy spectra, though differences emerge at higher energy levels. They also measured long-lived (up to one hour) metastable flux states.
method: The study uses microwave spectroscopy and single-shot readout of flux-dependent resonance frequencies and relaxation times. Theoretical modeling involves circuit quantization, symplectic geometry for the QPS model, and the Born-Oppenheimer approximation.
model_or_system: The physical system is an RF-SQUID containing a WSi nanowire weak link embedded in a three-dimensional copper cavity. The models considered are a Josephson junction with a skewed energy-phase relation and a quantum phase slip element acting as the electromagnetic dual.
key_observables: Resonance frequency (f) as a function of external magnetic flux, transmission amplitude (|S21|), and relaxation times of metastable persistent-current states.
important_parameters: Inductance ratio (beta), external magnetic flux (Phi_ext), skewness parameter (chi), kinetic inductance (Lk), and energy scales such as charging energy (Ec) and junction energy (Ej).
main_assumptions: The study employs a semiclassical approximation for resonance frequency calculations and uses the Born-Oppenheimer approximation to treat high-energy modes as decoupled.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device schematic and potential landscapes; Figure 2 and 3 present the experimental setup, device images, and flux-dependent resonance frequency data showing hysteresis; Figure 4 and 8 compare JJ and QPS models and energy spectra; Figure 9 shows the circuit models and numerical energy spectrum comparisons.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the need for nonlinearity in superconducting circuits, describes the fabrication and setup of the WSi RF-SQUID, presents experimental measurements of resonance frequency and hysteresis, compares the data against JJ and QPS theoretical models, and concludes with the measurement of metastable state lifetimes.
why_interesting: This work is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics because it explores the control of decoherence and relaxation in highly nonlinear, metastable quantum states and demonstrates a platform for engineering non-standard energy-phase relationships.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematics of a flux-tunable LC-circuit with a weak link (orange). The geometric inductance of the loop due to the su- perconducting wire (brown) is Lgeo, and the shunting capacitance is CB. The green region indicates the external flux Φext piercing the loop. (b) Two different circuit elements modeling the weak link: a JJ with current-phase relation I(φ) and energy-phase relation E(φ), or a QPS with voltage-charge relation V (q) and energy-charge relation E(q). (c) In the JJ-description, the potential from a sinusoidal CPR has a corrugated shape (dark blue), while a sawtooth CPR results in a similar shape but with much higher well barriers (dark red). FIG. 2. (a) RF-SQUID circuit coupled to a three-dimensional cop- per cavity. The RF-SQUID is patterned on a silicon chip suspended in a slot that is machined into the copper cavity and enclosed by an identical copper piece (not pictured). (b) Lumped element circuit di- agram. The red arrow refers to the coupling ga and gb between the cavity (orange) and the RF-SQUID (light green). The RF-SQUID de- vice is formed by a loop containing the weak link with energy EWSi and a parallel inductor Lp (pink). In addition, the device has a series inductor Ls (cyan), and a large capacitor CB. (c) Optical microscope image of the RF-SQUID device. The capacitor pads (yellow rectan- gles) are much larger...

FIG. 3. (a) A series of toy-model potential landscapes as the external flux evolves. The state of the system is indicated by a phase parti- cle (black dot) in a local minimum in each panel. (b) Transmission data showing the resonance frequency as a function of applied mag- netic field and frequency f. The colored hexagons at various flux values refer to the potentials in (a). In this data set, flux is increased from around −1.5 to 2.5 Φ0, with a phase jump occurring at approx- imately 1.56 Φ0, labeled as Φc ext. The curvature of the resonance frequency response is relatively flat rather than sinusoidal, suggest- ing a more sawtooth-like current phase relation [see Fig. 1(d)]. (c), (d)...
